Ground_Truth: Quicksort is a divide-and-conquer sorting algorithm whose running time depends on the balance of partitioning. In the worst case, it runs in Θ(n²), when the partition produces subproblems of sizes n−1 and 0. Under the assumption that all elements are distinct and all permutations are equally likely, its expected running time is Θ(n log n). The algorithm sorts in place and uses additional space proportional to the recursion depth.
Reference: Cormen, T. H., Leiserson, C. E., Rivest, R. L., & Stein, C. (2022). Introduction to Algorithms (4th ed.). MIT Press, pp. 182–184.

Ground_Truth: Dijkstra's algorithm solves the single-source shortest-path problem on a weighted directed graph with nonnegative edge weights. It maintains a set of vertices whose shortest-path distances from the source have been finalized and repeatedly selects the vertex with the minimum tentative distance using a priority queue, then relaxes its outgoing edges. Correctness relies on the property that once a vertex is extracted, its shortest-path distance is final, which holds only when all edge weights are nonnegative.
Reference: Cormen, T. H., Leiserson, C. E., Rivest, R. L., & Stein, C. (2022). Introduction to Algorithms (4th ed.). MIT Press, Section 22.3, pp 604-645.

Ground_Truth: Dynamic programming solves optimization problems by combining solutions to overlapping subproblems, typically using a bottom-up approach. A greedy algorithm constructs a solution by repeatedly making the locally optimal choice at each step in the hope of reaching a global optimum. Unlike dynamic programming, greedy algorithms do not rely on solutions to subproblems. Greedy algorithms do not always produce optimal solutions.
Reference: Cormen, T. H., Leiserson, C. E., Rivest, R. L., & Stein, C. (2022). Introduction to Algorithms (4th ed.). MIT Press, Chapter 14, pp. 365–370, Chapter 15, pp. 424, 427.

Ground_Truth: A hash table maps keys to array slots. A collision occurs when two or more keys hash to the same slot. The load factor α = N/M is the ratio of stored elements to table size. As α increases, the probability of collisions increases and performance degrades. Under uniform hashing and open addressing, the expected number of probes for an unsuccessful search is approximately 1/(1−α).
Reference: Cormen, T. H., Leiserson, C. E., Rivest, R. L., & Stein, C. (2022). Introduction to Algorithms (4th ed.). MIT Press, Chapter 11, pp 272-311.

Ground_Truth: An AVL tree is a height-balanced binary search tree in which, for every node, the heights of the left and right subtrees differ by at most one. This strict balance ensures O(log n) worst-case time for search, insertion, and deletion, but may require more rotations during updates. A red-black tree is a self-balancing binary search tree that maintains balance using color-based properties (red and black nodes), guaranteeing O(log n) worst-case time for search, insertion, and deletion, typically with fewer rotations than AVL trees at the cost of a potentially higher tree height.
Reference: Cormen, T. H., Leiserson, C. E., Rivest, R. L., & Stein, C. (2022). Introduction to Algorithms (4th ed.). MIT Press. AVL trees: Problem 13-3, pp. 358–360. Red-black trees: Chapter 13, pp. 331–359.

Ground_Truth: Breadth-First Search (BFS) explores a graph level by level using a queue to maintain the frontier. Its auxiliary space complexity is O(|V|), as the queue may store up to all vertices in the worst case. Depth-First Search (DFS) explores as far as possible along each branch before backtracking, using a stack or recursion. Its auxiliary space complexity is O(|V|) in the worst case due to the recursion stack or explicit stack.
Reference: Cormen, T. H., Leiserson, C. E., Rivest, R. L., & Stein, C. (2022). Introduction to Algorithms (4th ed.). MIT Press, Chapter 20, pp. 549–584.

Ground_Truth: Space complexity measures the amount of memory required by an algorithm as a function of input size, including both input storage and auxiliary space used during execution. Algorithm design often involves trade-offs between time and space, where additional memory can be used to improve running time (e.g., storing intermediate results), while reducing memory usage may increase running time.
Reference: Cormen, T. H., Leiserson, C. E., Rivest, R. L., & Stein, C. (2022). Introduction to Algorithms (4th ed.). MIT Press, Chapter 1, pp. 5–16.

Ground_Truth: Inheritance is a mechanism in object-oriented programming that allows a class (subtype) to inherit properties and behavior from another class (base type), enabling code reuse and hierarchical relationships. The Liskov Substitution Principle (LSP) states that subtypes must be substitutable for their base types without altering the correctness of the program. This requires that inherited behavior is preserved, including method contracts, invariants, and preconditions/postconditions.
Reference: Liskov, B. H.; Wing, J. M. (1994). 'A Behavioral Notion of Subtyping'. ACM TOPLAS, 16(6), pp. 1811–1841. / Martin, R. C. (2017). Clean Architecture. Prentice Hall. pp. 140-145

Ground_Truth: An interface defines a type by specifying a set of methods that a class must implement, without requiring instance state, while an abstract class can provide both method implementations and instance fields. Interfaces enable multiple inheritance of type, whereas abstract classes support code reuse through shared implementation but allow only single inheritance. Interfaces are generally preferred for defining types, while abstract classes are useful when sharing common code among closely related classes.
Reference: Bloch, J. (2018). Effective Java (3rd ed.). Addison-Wesley, Item 20, pp. 99–107.

Ground_Truth: Lexical scope means that scope is defined by author-time decisions of where functions are declared. The lexing phase of compilation is able to know where and how all identifiers are declared, and thus predict how they will be looked up during execution. Closure is when a function is able to remember and access its lexical scope even when that function is executing outside its lexical scope.
Reference: Simpson, K. (2014). You Don't Know JS: Scope & Closures. O'Reilly Media, Chapter 2 (Lexical Scope) and Chapter 5 (Scope Closure), pp 47-52.

Concept: Static vs Dynamic typing and type safety
Ground_Truth: Static typing performs type checking at compile time, using static analysis of the program to approximate runtime behavior and detect type errors before execution. Dynamic typing performs type checking at runtime, where variables are not bound to fixed types and type errors are detected during program execution. Type safety is the property that well-typed programs do not produce undefined or invalid behavior, such as applying operations to incompatible types.
Reference: Pierce, B. C. (2002). Types and Programming Languages. MIT Press, Chapter 1: Introduction.

Ground_Truth: Use checked exceptions for conditions from which the caller can reasonably be expected to recover. Use runtime exceptions (unchecked) to indicate programming errors, typically precondition violations where the client failed to adhere to the API's contract. Errors are reserved for JVM-level failures and should not be caught or subclassed.
Reference: Bloch, J. (2018). Effective Java (3rd ed.). Addison-Wesley, Item 70, pp. 297–300.

Concept: Garbage Collection: Mark-and-Sweep vs Reference Counting
Ground_Truth: Reference counting associates a count of references with each object and reclaims memory when the count reaches zero, but it cannot collect cyclic garbage. The mark-and-sweep algorithm identifies reachable objects by tracing from root references, marks all reachable objects, and reclaims memory occupied by unmarked objects. Unlike reference counting, mark-and-sweep can collect cyclic garbage because it determines reachability through graph traversal rather than reference counts.
Reference: Jones, R.; Hosking, A.; Moss, E. (2023). The Garbage Collection Handbook: The Art of Automatic Memory Management (2nd ed.). CRC Press, Chapter 2 (Mark-Sweep) and Chapter 5 (Reference Counting).

Ground_Truth: Multithreading is a form of concurrency that uses multiple threads of execution managed by the operating system to perform tasks in parallel or overlapping time periods. Asynchronous programming is a form of concurrency in which operations are executed without blocking the calling thread, typically by representing ongoing work as tasks that can be awaited or completed later. Unlike multithreading, asynchronous programming does not require a dedicated thread for each concurrent operation.
Reference: Cleary, S. (2019). Concurrency in C# Cookbook (2nd ed.). O'Reilly Media, Chapter 1.

Ground_Truth: To preserve data integrity, a database system must ensure: Atomicity—either all operations of a transaction are reflected in the database or none are; Consistency—a committed transaction preserves all defined integrity constraints, moving the database from one valid state to another; Isolation—concurrent transactions execute without interfering with each other, as if executed serially, with intermediate results hidden; Durability—once a transaction commits, its changes persist despite system failures. Consistency refers to maintaining database integrity rules, while Isolation refers to controlling interaction between concurrent transactions.
Reference: Silberschatz, A., Korth, H. F., & Sudarshan, S. (2019). Database System Concepts (7th ed.). McGraw-Hill, Chapter 17: Transactions.

Ground_Truth: Normalization organizes data to reduce redundancy and improve integrity. First Normal Form (1NF): All attributes contain atomic (indivisible) values, and there are no repeating groups. Second Normal Form (2NF): The relation is in 1NF and every non-key attribute is fully functionally dependent on the entire primary key (no partial dependencies). Third Normal Form (3NF): The relation is in 2NF and has no transitive dependencies; non-key attributes depend only on candidate keys. Normalization to 3NF eliminates common insertion, update, and deletion anomalies and is widely considered a practical design goal, although higher normal forms (e.g., BCNF) may be required in some cases.
Reference: Date, C. J. (2004). An Introduction to Database Systems (8th ed.). Pearson/Addison Wesley, Chapter 12: Further Normalization I: 1NF, 2NF, 3NF, BCNF, pp. 349–388.

Ground_Truth: The SQL JOIN clause combines rows from two or more tables based on a specified join condition. INNER JOIN returns only rows for which the join condition evaluates to true in both tables. LEFT OUTER JOIN returns all rows from the left table and matching rows from the right table; for non-matching rows, attributes from the right table are set to NULL. RIGHT OUTER JOIN returns all rows from the right table and matching rows from the left table; for non-matching rows, attributes from the left table are set to NULL. FULL OUTER JOIN returns all rows from both tables; rows are matched where the join condition holds, and non-matching rows from either side are padded with NULL values.
Reference: Garcia-Molina, H., Ullman, J. D., & Widom, J. (2009). Database Systems: The Complete Book (2nd ed.). Pearson, Chapter 6.3.6–6.3.8: SQL Join Expressions and Outerjoins.

Ground_Truth: A B-tree index may not be used or may not improve query performance when the query has low selectivity (i.e., it returns a large fraction of the table), in which case a full table scan can be more efficient than index-based access due to the cost of random disk I/O. The index may also be ineffective if the query applies functions, expressions, or negation to the indexed column, preventing efficient index lookup, or if the table is very small. Database query optimizers use cost-based analysis and table statistics to determine whether to use an index.
Reference: Garcia-Molina, H., Ullman, J. D., & Widom, J. (2009). Database Systems: The Complete Book (2nd ed.). Pearson, Chapter 14: Index Structures. / Silberschatz, A., Korth, H. F., & Sudarshan, S. (2019). Database System Concepts (7th ed.). McGraw-Hill, Chapter 14: Indexing.

Concept: Transaction isolation levels: Read Committed vs Serializable
Ground_Truth: READ COMMITTED ensures that a transaction reads only data that has been committed by other transactions; however, successive reads within the same transaction may return different results, allowing non-repeatable reads and phantom reads. SERIALIZABLE ensures that concurrent transactions execute with results equivalent to some serial schedule, preventing dirty reads, non-repeatable reads, and phantom reads. Higher isolation levels improve correctness guarantees but typically reduce concurrency.
Reference: Silberschatz, A., Korth, H. F., & Sudarshan, S. (2019). Database System Concepts (7th ed.). McGraw-Hill, Chapter 17: Transactions.

Ground_Truth: The CAP theorem states that in the presence of a network partition, a distributed system must choose between Consistency and Availability. Consistency means that all nodes see the same data at the same time (i.e., every read reflects the most recent write). Availability means that every request receives a response, regardless of the state of individual nodes. Partition tolerance means that the system continues to operate despite network partitions (communication failures between nodes). Since network partitions are unavoidable in distributed systems, CAP is commonly interpreted as a trade-off between Consistency (CP) and Availability (AP) under partition conditions.
Reference: Silberschatz, A., Korth, H. F., & Sudarshan, S. (2019). Database System Concepts (7th ed.). McGraw-Hill, Chapter 19: Distributed Databases.

Ground_Truth: Denormalization is the process of intentionally introducing redundancy into a normalized relational schema to improve read performance by reducing the need for join operations. It is a deliberate trade-off, commonly used in read-heavy workloads (e.g., reporting or data warehousing), where the cost of joins outweighs the overhead of maintaining redundant data. While denormalization can significantly accelerate SELECT queries, it increases storage requirements, complicates write operations (INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE), and introduces the risk of data inconsistency due to redundant representations.
Reference: Garcia-Molina, H., Ullman, J. D., & Widom, J. (2009). Database Systems: The Complete Book (2nd ed.). Pearson. / Silberschatz, A., Korth, H. F., & Sudarshan, S. (2019). Database System Concepts (7th ed.). McGraw-Hill.

Ground_Truth: The TCP three-way handshake establishes a connection and synchronizes sequence numbers between client and server. (1) The client sends a TCP segment with the SYN flag set and a pseudo-random initial sequence number (client_isn), entering the SYN_SENT state. (2) The server responds with a SYN-ACK segment containing its own pseudo-random initial sequence number (server_isn) and an acknowledgment number of client_isn+1, entering the SYN_RCVD state. (3) The client replies with an ACK segment with acknowledgment number server_isn+1. Both sides then transition to the ESTABLISHED state. The handshake confirms bidirectional communication and initializes sequence number spaces for reliable data transfer.
Reference: Kurose, J. F., & Ross, K. W. (2020). Computer Networking: A Top-Down Approach (8th ed.). Pearson, Chapter 3.5: TCP Connection Management, pp. 240–246.

Ground_Truth: The OSI model defines a seven-layer architecture: Physical, Data Link, Network, Transport, Session, Presentation, and Application. The TCP/IP model defines a four-layer architecture: Link (Network Interface), Internet, Transport, and Application. The TCP/IP model consolidates the OSI Physical and Data Link layers into the Link layer, and the OSI Session and Presentation layers into the Application layer. While the OSI model serves as a conceptual reference framework, the TCP/IP model reflects the practical architecture of the Internet protocol suite.
Reference: Kurose, J. F., & Ross, K. W. (2020). Computer Networking: A Top-Down Approach (8th ed.). Pearson, Chapter 1.5

Ground_Truth: DNS resolution can be recursive or iterative. In recursive resolution, a DNS server resolves the query on behalf of the client, contacting other servers as needed and returning the final answer. In iterative resolution, each DNS server returns the best information it has (often a referral), and the client (or resolver) continues querying other servers. Recursive queries shift the resolution burden to the server, while iterative queries distribute it across multiple queries.
Reference: Kurose, J. F., & Ross, K. W. (2020). Computer Networking: A Top-Down Approach (8th ed.). Pearson, Chapter 2.4: DNS, pp. 168–175.

Ground_Truth: Classless Inter-Domain Routing (CIDR) represents IP addresses using a prefix of arbitrary length, written as a.b.c.d/x, where x indicates the number of bits in the network portion. CIDR enables flexible subnetting and efficient allocation of IP address space by eliminating fixed class boundaries. Routers use the longest prefix match rule to determine the forwarding path for a packet.
Reference: Kurose, J. F., & Ross, K. W. (2020). Computer Networking: A Top-Down Approach (8th ed.). Pearson, Chapter 4.4: IP Addressing, pp. 330–336

Ground_Truth: HTTP methods differ in semantics and idempotency. A method is idempotent if multiple identical requests have the same effect as a single request. GET, PUT, and DELETE are idempotent: repeated requests do not change the result beyond the initial application. POST is not idempotent, as repeated requests can create multiple resources or cause repeated side effects. Idempotency is important for reliability and retry mechanisms in distributed systems.
Reference: Kurose, J. F., & Ross, K. W. (2020). Computer Networking: A Top-Down Approach (8th ed.). Pearson, Chapter 2.2: The Web and HTTP, pp. 122–130.

Ground_Truth: Distance-vector routing algorithms (e.g., RIP) compute shortest paths based on information from neighboring routers, iteratively updating distance estimates using distributed Bellman-Ford equations. Link-state routing algorithms (e.g., OSPF) require each router to have a complete view of the network topology and compute shortest paths using Dijkstra's algorithm. Distance-vector approaches are simpler but slower to converge and susceptible to routing loops, while link-state approaches converge faster and provide more accurate routing at the cost of higher complexity.
Reference: Kurose, J. F., & Ross, K. W. (2020). Computer Networking: A Top-Down Approach (8th ed.). Pearson, Chapter 5.3–5.4: Routing Algorithms, pp. 365–385.

Ground_Truth: TLS handshake protocols establish a secure connection by negotiating cryptographic parameters and exchanging keys. In RSA-based key exchange, the client encrypts a premaster secret with the server's public key, allowing the server to derive the session keys. In Diffie-Hellman (DH) key exchange, both parties contribute to a shared secret without transmitting it directly, providing forward secrecy. Modern TLS implementations prefer (EC)DHE over RSA key exchange due to improved security properties.
Reference: Kurose, J. F., & Ross, K. W. (2020). Computer Networking: A Top-Down Approach (8th ed.). Pearson, Chapter 8.3: Secure Sockets Layer / TLS, pp. 712–720.

Ground_Truth: A process is a program in execution with its own memory space, resources, and execution context. A thread is a lightweight execution unit within a process that shares the same address space and resources but has its own program counter, registers, and stack. Threads within the same process can communicate more efficiently than separate processes due to shared memory.
Reference: Silberschatz, A., Galvin, P. B., & Gagne, G. (2018). Operating System Concepts (10th ed.). Wiley, Chapter 4: Threads, pp. 163–172.

Ground_Truth: Context switching is the process of saving the state of a currently running process or thread and restoring the state of another, enabling multitasking. The operating system saves CPU registers, program counter, and memory mapping information of the current process and loads the corresponding state of the next scheduled process.
Reference: Silberschatz, A., Galvin, P. B., & Gagne, G. (2018). Operating System Concepts (10th ed.). Wiley, Chapter 5: CPU Scheduling, pp. 189–194.

Ground_Truth: CPU scheduling determines which process runs next. FCFS executes processes in arrival order. SJF selects the process with the shortest expected CPU burst time, minimizing average waiting time but risking starvation. Round Robin assigns a fixed time quantum to each process, ensuring fairness and responsiveness in time-sharing systems.
Reference: Silberschatz, A., Galvin, P. B., & Gagne, G. (2018). Operating System Concepts (10th ed.). Wiley, Chapter 5: CPU Scheduling, pp. 198–210.

Ground_Truth: A deadlock occurs when a set of processes are permanently blocked. It can arise only if all four Coffman conditions hold simultaneously: mutual exclusion, hold and wait, no preemption, and circular wait. Breaking any one of these conditions prevents deadlock.
Reference: Silberschatz, A., Galvin, P. B., & Gagne, G. (2018). Operating System Concepts (10th ed.). Wiley, Chapter 7: Deadlocks, pp. 312–318.

Ground_Truth: Virtual memory allows execution of processes that may not be fully loaded into physical memory. Paging divides memory into fixed-size pages mapped to frames in physical memory. A page table translates virtual addresses to physical addresses, enabling isolation and efficient memory utilization.
Reference: Silberschatz, A., Galvin, P. B., & Gagne, G. (2018). Operating System Concepts (10th ed.). Wiley, Chapter 9: Virtual Memory, pp. 356–365.

Ground_Truth: Paging divides memory into fixed-size blocks, eliminating external fragmentation but possibly causing internal fragmentation. Segmentation divides memory into variable-sized logical units such as functions or data structures, improving logical organization but suffering from external fragmentation. Modern systems often combine both approaches.
Reference: Tanenbaum, A. S., & Bos, H. (2015). Modern Operating Systems (4th ed.). Pearson, Chapter 3: Memory Management, pp. 143–150.

Ground_Truth: A semaphore is a synchronization primitive used to control access to shared resources. A counting semaphore allows multiple concurrent accesses up to a defined limit, while a binary semaphore (mutex) allows only one. Semaphores prevent race conditions by enforcing mutual exclusion and coordination between concurrent processes.
Reference: Silberschatz, A., Galvin, P. B., & Gagne, G. (2018). Operating System Concepts (10th ed.). Wiley, Chapter 6: Synchronization, pp. 246–255.
